does: This paper develops a method for compiling procedural workflows directly into the weights of fine-tuned small language models rather than maintaining them in external orchestration frameworks. The authors demonstrate that workflows with up to 14 nodes can be encoded into model parameters, achieving near-frontier model quality on procedural tasks while reducing inference costs by two orders of magnitude. They address practical barriers to adoption of this compiled-agent approach by providing empirical validation across multiple domains including travel booking and customer support, showing that the technique produces competitive results without requiring frontier models or large context windows for workflow specification.
connects: This work builds on recent findings that procedural task workflows can be embedded directly in frontier model system prompts more effectively than through external orchestration, as shown in Dennis et al. (2026a). It extends prior research on workflow compilation including SimpleTOD, FireAct, SynTOD, WorkflowLLM, and Agent Lumos that established the technical feasibility of weight-based workflow encoding. The paper also responds to the proliferation of agent orchestration frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI, Google ADK, OpenAI Agents SDK, Semantic Kernel, Strands, LlamaIndex) that have become dominant despite not being optimal for procedural tasks, identifying why developers have overlooked the compiled-agent approach despite its theoretical advantages.
enables: This work enables practical deployment of specialized agents that are cheaper to run, don't expose proprietary workflows to external providers, and don't consume limited context windows, potentially shifting developer practices away from external orchestration toward embedded procedural reasoning. It opens directions for scaling workflow compilation to more complex multi-node systems, exploring tradeoffs between workflow complexity and model size, and developing better methods for incorporating domain knowledge directly into fine-tuned weights. The approach could enable new applications where cost and privacy constraints previously made agent-based systems impractical, particularly for enterprises requiring confidential procedure handling and high-volume inference at lower expense than frontier models.
